planting.note: Plant bulb or container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 7–10; Spice Lily is a small native bulbous perennial of south Texas and northeastern Mexico — forming compact rosettes of narrow, purple-spotted, semi-succulent leaves and producing tall flower spikes of tubular, brownish-purple to greenish flowers with a remarkably strong, sweet spice fragrance strongest at dusk — an outstanding hummingbird and hawk moth nectar plant.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline-tolerant soil in full sun; Manfreda maculosa is adapted to shallow, calcareous, rocky soils of the Texas Edwards Plateau and Rio Grande Plains — excellent drainage is critical; avoid clay and overwatering; requires no supplemental water once established in appropriate xeric conditions. Manfreda maculosa (Spice Lily or Mottled Tuberose) is a compact, bulbous perennial in the Asparagaceae family native to the rocky, calcareous soils and cedar-oak scrub of the Edwards Plateau and Rio Grande Plains of south Texas and extending into Nuevo Leon and Tamaulipas, Mexico. The plant forms ground-level rosettes of narrow, strap-like, gray-green leaves beautifully decorated with irregular dark-purple spots and blotches — giving an ornamental mottled appearance similar to its relative M. virginica. In summer, slender, branching flower spikes emerge to 3–5 feet, bearing small, brownish-green to brownish-purple, tubular flowers that are powerfully and sweetly fragrant — a spicy-sweet scent strongest at dusk and into the night, designed to attract hawk moths (Sphingidae) as pollinators alongside hummingbirds. Like M. virginica, it is not monocarpic and blooms reliably each year. An outstanding native accent plant for rock gardens, dry borders, and xeriscape plantings in zones 7–10 across Texas and the Southwest.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Manfreda maculosa
Texas Native Plant Society — Manfreda maculosa

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 8–10; Chamburo is a drought-adapted, dryland papaya relative native to the dry intermontane valleys of the Andean highlands of Ecuador and northern Peru (200–2,800m elevation) — producing oblong, yellow-green, aromatic fruits the size of an egg with white papaya-like flesh and a mild, sweet, papaya-lemon aroma, cooked in Ecuadorian traditional cuisine.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, dry to moderately moist soil; Vasconcellea candicans is adapted to dry inter-Andean valley conditions and is considerably more drought tolerant than common papaya — water moderately and avoid overwatering or waterlogged soils. Vasconcellea candicans (Chamburo or White-flowering Papaya) is a small tree in the Caricaceae family native to the dry inter-Andean valleys and hillside scrub of Ecuador and northern Peru at elevations of 200 to 2,800 meters — adapted to significantly drier conditions than most Vasconcellea species, which are predominantly cloud-forest plants. The white-flowered trees (the species name 'candicans' means 'becoming white' in reference to the flowers) produce clusters of elongated, oval, pale-yellow to white-green, fragrant fruits about the size of a large egg, with soft, white, papaya-textured flesh and a mild, sweet-sour, papaya-lemon-vanilla aromatic flavor. Chamburo fruits are traditionally cooked in Ecuadorian highland cuisine — used in the classic dish cuy al horno (roasted guinea pig with vegetable accompaniments) and in fruit preserves and juices. The plant tolerates dry conditions, shallow soils, and light frost considerably better than common papaya, making it potentially useful for drier zone 8b–10 gardens in the southwestern United States and similar Mediterranean climates. Dioecious; plant male and female trees.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Coyure Palm (Ruffle Palm) is an ornamental, small to medium palm native to the tropical forests and forest edges of the Lesser Antilles and northern South America (Venezuela, Trinidad) — armed with long, black, needle-like spines on trunk and fronds, but producing distinctive, ruffled, bright-green fronds and bright-red ornamental fruits that make it a dramatic specimen plant for tropical gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun — handle with thick gloves as all parts of the trunk and fronds are densely armed with long, sharp, black spines; plant where foot traffic will not encounter the spines. Aiphanes minima (Coyure Palm, Ruffle Palm, or Macaw Palm) is a small to medium, solitary-trunked palm native to the humid tropical forests of the Lesser Antilles (Dominica, Guadeloupe, Martinique) and Trinidad and Venezuela, belonging to the heavily armed Aiphanes genus whose characteristic feature — needle-like, jet-black spines covering the trunk, leaf sheaths, and petioles — makes the palm spectacular but demanding of cautious handling. Despite the forbidding spination, the palm is genuinely ornamental: the fronds are distinctively somewhat irregular and ruffled in their leaflet arrangement (the 'ruffle' appearance) with a fresh, vivid-green color, and the fruits are brilliant red-orange, round, about the size of a cherry — edible with sweet, starchy flesh in its native range and consumed by macaws and other parrots, which explains the common name 'Macaw Palm' in parts of its range. Aiphanes minima is shade-tolerant as a young plant and grows well under the partial shade of taller trees in tropical landscapes. An unusual and dramatic specimen for collectors' gardens in zone 10–12.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Aiphanes collection
Flora of the Lesser Antilles — Aiphanes minima

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, lean to average, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 7–10; Brazilian Needle Palm is among the most cold-hardy of all South American palms — native to the campos of southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina, tolerating temperatures to 12–18°F when established — with stiff, blue-green fan fronds and a distinctive trunk covered in persistent, matted fiber and needle-like spines.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to average soil; Trithrinax brasiliensis is drought tolerant and adapted to poor, dry grassland soils of the South American campos — requires excellent drainage; water regularly only in establishment, then minimal supplemental water needed in appropriate climates. Trithrinax brasiliensis (Brazilian Needle Palm or Spiny Trunk Palm) is an attractive, slow-growing fan palm native to the subtropical campos (savanna grasslands) of Rio Grande do Sul in southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina, one of the most cold-tolerant fan palms available from South America. The trunk is distinctive: covered in a dense mat of persistent leaf-base fibers and needle-like spines up to several inches long that give it a rough, armored texture. The stiff, flat fan fronds — divided into narrow segments — have a blue-green to silvery-green color that contrasts beautifully with the dark trunk. Cold hardiness is the plant's outstanding horticultural characteristic: established specimens survive temperatures of 12–18°F (zone 7b–8a) when planted in well-drained soils, making it one of the very few ornamental palms suitable for garden conditions in zone 7. Drought tolerant once established. A valuable, unusual cold-hardy palm for large landscape gardens in the South and Southwest United States where an extremely exotic, palm-like accent plant is desired in zone 7 or warmer climates.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Black Palm is a rare, monotypic Australian rainforest palm endemic to the Wet Tropics of tropical Queensland — producing a slender, elegantly ringed, dark-colored trunk topped by gracefully arching, bright-green pinnate fronds, highly ornamental in form, and producing a hard, dense heartwood used in traditional Queensland joinery and woodworking.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Normanbya normanbyi requires consistent moisture and protection from cold, dry winds — mulch the root zone deeply and water regularly; it is not tolerant of drought or frost. Normanbya normanbyi (Black Palm or Queensland Black Palm) is a monotypic genus — the single species Normanbya normanbyi — a slender, elegant, solitary-trunked feather palm endemic to the tropical rainforests of the Wet Tropics bioregion in far-north Queensland, Australia (Cairns to Cooktown), where it occurs in lowland rainforest and riparian gallery forest. The common name 'Black Palm' references the heartwood — the dense, dark-brown to near-black inner wood of the trunk — one of the heaviest, densest, and most durable timbers of any Australian native palm or plant material, historically used by the Yidinjdji and Djabugay peoples for tool making and by early European settlers for fine joinery, flooring, and specialty woodworking. As a landscape palm, Normanbya normanbyi is highly ornamental: the slender, prominently ring-marked trunk (the rings from old leaf scars create a distinctive pattern) supports a graceful, open crown of arching pinnate fronds with attractively silvered frond undersides. It is one of Australia's rarest and most restricted palms in the wild and is protected under Queensland state legislation. An excellent specimen palm for tropical zone 10–12 landscapes with ample moisture.
sources: Queensland Government — Normanbya normanbyi species profile
Australian National Botanic Gardens — Normanbya normanbyi
Royal Botanic Garden Sydney — Australian Palms collection

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Christmas Palm (Manila Palm, Adonidia Palm) is the most widely planted small landscape palm in Florida, the Caribbean, and tropical gardens worldwide — a compact, fast-growing, elegant Philippine native that produces brilliant clusters of shiny-red fruits ripening at Christmas time, making it one of the most recognizable and beloved ornamental palms in subtropical landscapes.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Veitchia merrillii is fast-growing for a palm and relatively tolerant of brief dry periods once established — fertilize with palm-specific slow-release fertilizer containing magnesium and potassium twice per year; very sensitive to cold below 32°F. Veitchia merrillii (Christmas Palm, Manila Palm, or Adonidia Palm, syn. Adonidia merrillii) is a slender, elegant, single-trunked feather palm native to the tropical forests of the Philippine archipelago, and unquestionably the single most planted small ornamental palm in South Florida, the Caribbean, and tropical landscape gardens worldwide. The appeal is immediate: a compact, tidy, self-cleaning, fast-growing palm that matures at 10–25 feet — ideal for residential lots — with a smooth, prominently ringed trunk, a well-defined crown shaft, and gracefully arching, bright-green pinnate fronds. The most distinctive seasonal feature is the clusters of brilliant, scarlet-red to crimson fruits that ripen in November through December, cascading beneath the crown in spectacular holiday-colored display that gave the palm its name. Veitchia merrillii adapts to a wide range of well-drained soils including South Florida's alkaline limestone, tolerates salt spray better than many palms, and grows well in full sun with regular irrigation. Susceptible to Lethal Yellowing disease in Florida; choose resistant 'Maypan' hybrids or treat prophylactically with oxytetracycline injections in endemic areas.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Veitchia merrillii (Christmas Palm)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Palm Collection
